one_line_takeaway: Lipschitz-based robustness certifiers that assume real-arithmetic execution -- including formally-verified ones -- can be fooled by floating-point rounding, sometimes at semantically meaningful radii under adversarial weight construction; this paper gives a Rocq-mechanised, floating-point-sound alternative that closes the gap in all tested cases while remaining practical (milliseconds per instance, full-test-set certification) except on CIFAR-10, where the cost is driven by the task's large input-radius-to-perturbation ratio rather than model size.
key_finding: RQ1 (soundness): across all 8 model-format combinations tested (float16/float32/float64, excluding CIFAR-10 float16 which violates a precondition) and all 3 adversarially-biased model variants, the floating-point-aware certifier rejects 100% of the counterexamples that a real-arithmetic certifier (including Tobler et al.'s formally-verified one) wrongly certifies as robust. RQ2 (cost): Standard-mode floating-point-sound certification is non-vacuous, certifying 76.8% (HIGGS) to 95.5% (MNIST) of test points at milliseconds per instance; the drop in certified rate versus real-arithmetic is small on 5 of 6 tasks (0.24pp MNIST, 1.16pp EMNIST-ByClass, 1.49pp Fashion-MNIST, 3.69pp EMNIST-Balanced, 6.12pp HIGGS) but 27.2pp on CIFAR-10 -- shown (via a 'conservatism ratio' tied to r0/epsilon: 0.08 for EMNIST-ByClass, 0.17 for HIGGS-512, 1.09 for CIFAR-10) to be driven by the certification task's input-radius-to-perturbation ratio (r0/epsilon = 207 for CIFAR-10 vs. 35 for the architecturally-identical EMNIST-ByClass), not by network size. The Gram-iteration spectral-norm computation for CIFAR-10 takes 19.5s versus 'tens of hours' for Tobler et al.'s exact-rational computation. RQ3: the pre-deployment Hybrid-Centre/Measured-Radii modes reclaim most reclaimable degradation, e.g. CIFAR-10 VRA rises from 16.96% (Standard) to 27.52% (Measured-Radii) against a 35.95% real-arithmetic ceiling; against ERAN on 100-point samples the paper's Measured-Radii mode ties ERAN on MNIST (98% vs 99%) and EMNIST-ByClass (81% vs 80%) and beats it on Fashion-MNIST (80% vs 53%) and EMNIST-Balanced (81% vs 64%), while ERAN wins only on CIFAR-10 (47% vs 40% -- even above the paper's real-arithmetic ceiling, attributed to DeepPoly's tighter per-neuron relaxation on that task). RQ4: the one-off Gram-norm computation grows roughly quadratically with width (~4x per width doubling); per-instance certification stays a few milliseconds and scales roughly linearly with width but is largely insensitive to class count (62-class EMNIST-ByClass costs 3.5ms vs. 4.2ms for 2-class HIGGS-1024).
core_contribution: The paper (Toby Murray, University of Melbourne) shows that Lipschitz-based (global) robustness certification for feed-forward ReLU networks -- e.g. the method of Leino et al., as implemented in Tobler et al.'s formally-verified real-arithmetic certifier -- is unsound once the certified network is actually executed in floating point rather than real arithmetic. The author (1) constructs concrete counterexamples where a formally-verified real-arithmetic certifier certifies a point x0 as epsilon-robust, yet a nearby point x1 within epsilon of x0 is classified differently under float32 execution due to rounding; (2) develops a compositional, mechanised (Rocq/Coq, on top of the LAProof floating-point linear-algebra library) theory that (a) derives sufficient conditions for absence of overflow across an entire epsilon-ball, (b) derives a layer-by-layer forward bound on the deviation between real and floating-point network execution, and (c) uses that bound to produce a modified, floating-point-sound certification condition that quantifies certificate degradation; (3) gives a floating-point Gram-iteration algorithm for computing spectral-norm upper bounds that is proved never to under-estimate the true norm, and is far cheaper than exact-rational computation; and (4) introduces two pre-deployment modes (Hybrid-Centre and Measured-Radii) that use a high-precision (float64) reference execution, available offline but not at inference time, to reclaim much of the certified-robustness loss caused by floating-point-soundness. All numbered theorems are mechanised in Rocq 8.20.1; the executable Python certifier used for the empirical evaluation is a separate, unverified implementation whose real-arithmetic kernel was validated by reproducing Tobler et al.'s verified certifier's outputs on their three image models.
threat_model: Two threat models are used. (1) A benign/non-adversarial setting: for naturally trained models, floating-point rounding alone (no attacker-controlled weights) can already cause a certifier that assumes real arithmetic to mis-certify robustness -- the discovered counterexample radii are tiny (about 4x10^-8 to 2x10^-6 across MNIST/Fashion-MNIST/CIFAR-10 at float32), so this is presented as a soundness gap rather than a practical attack surface on its own. (2) An explicit adversarial-weights threat model: an attacker who controls the model's parameters (not the input) injects a large compensating bias pair (a flat bias B=10^6 at the second-to-last hidden layer, cancelled by a compensating output-layer bias) that leaves the real-arithmetic Lipschitz constant and certification unchanged (biases don't affect the Lipschitz constant) while inflating intermediate floating-point rounding error at the output layer. This amplifies the floating-point/real-arithmetic gap up to semantically meaningful perturbation radii (epsilon up to 0.37 on MNIST, 25 of 30 generated counterexamples exceeding epsilon=0.1) while barely changing clean test accuracy (98.23% vs. 98.40% original). This is a backdoor-style attack on the certifier's soundness guarantee, not a training-data or inference-time attack on the model's predictions themselves.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the author: (1) the theory assumes 'FP-exact' activations (agreement between floating-point and real-arithmetic evaluation), which covers ReLU/identity/hard-tanh/abs but excludes smooth activations like sigmoid/tanh whose floating-point implementations introduce their own rounding -- left to future work; (2) at float16, robustness certification becomes vacuous (no test points certified robust under any mode), because the worst-case dot-product accumulation error term blows up as n*u approaches 1; the author suggests wider (e.g. float32) accumulators for float16 data as a plausible fix; (3) bfloat16 is excluded from evaluation entirely because the practical n_l*u << 1 requirement fails; (4) the Rocq-mechanised theory covers round-to-nearest/gradual-underflow semantics; extension to flush-to-zero semantics is shown to require only changed absolute-error constants but 'is not yet mechanised'; (5) the executable Python certifier itself is unverified -- only its real-arithmetic kernel was validated against Tobler et al.'s verified certifier's outputs, and the floating-point extensions are 'carefully audited' rather than proven; verified extraction of the certifier is left as future work; (6) an adversary who controls model weights can deliberately force vacuity (0% certified) via the compensating-bias construction -- the paper argues this is 'sound' behavior (correctly refusing to certify) rather than a limitation of the theory, but it does mean floating-point-sound certification offers no robustness guarantee at all against such adversarially constructed models; (7) generative AI (Claude Code and ChatGPT) was used throughout for developing the mathematics, the Rocq formalisation, the Python implementation, and drafting, under the author's stated review and auditing.

quantitative_results: RQ1: floating-point-aware certifier rejected 100% of counterexamples across 8 model-format combinations (float16/32/64) and 3 adversarially-biased variants, each with at least 30 counterexamples
MNIST adversarial-bias attack: 30 counterexamples generated at float32, epsilon up to 0.37, 25/30 exceeding epsilon=0.1; adversarial model retained 98.23% test accuracy vs 98.40% for the original
Naturally-trained-model counterexample radii at float32: roughly 4e-8 to 2e-6 across MNIST/Fashion-MNIST/CIFAR-10; at float16 up to ~0.01; at float64 near format resolution (~1e-15)

Certified rate (Std mode, Table 1): MNIST 95.50%, Fashion-MNIST 82.16%, CIFAR-10 18.90%, HIGGS 76.76%, EMNIST-ByClass 85.01%, EMNIST-Balanced 74.06%, vs. real-arithmetic ceilings of 95.74%, 83.65%, 46.12%, 82.88%, 86.17%, 77.75% respectively
Floating-point cost (drop from real to Std certified rate): 0.24pp MNIST, 1.49pp Fashion-MNIST, 27.2pp CIFAR-10 (outlier), 6.12pp HIGGS, 1.16pp EMNIST-ByClass, 3.69pp EMNIST-Balanced
Conservatism ratio (mean FP margin degradation / mean real-arithmetic margin): EMNIST-ByClass 0.08 (r0/eps=35), HIGGS-512 0.17 (r0/eps=52), CIFAR-10 1.09 (r0/eps=207, ~14x EMNIST-ByClass's ratio)

Spectral-norm (Gram-12) computation time: MNIST 5.5s, Fashion-MNIST 10.3s, CIFAR-10 19.5s, HIGGS-1024 192.4s, EMNIST-ByClass 18.4s, EMNIST-Balanced 90.8s -- CIFAR-10's 19.5s vs 'tens of hours' for prior exact-rational computation
Per-instance certification time (Table 2, ms): Std mode from 2.09ms (MNIST) to 7.69ms (EMNIST-Balanced); Hyb and Meas modes add roughly 1-2x more

Pre-deployment VRA reclamation: CIFAR-10 16.96% (Std) -> 27.52% (Meas) against 35.95% real ceiling; HIGGS 59.13% -> 61.97% against 62.69% ceiling; EMNIST-Balanced 68.33% -> 70.06% against 71.25% ceiling

ERAN comparison (100-point sample, VRA): MNIST 98% (Meas) vs 99% (ERAN); Fashion-MNIST 80% vs 53%; CIFAR-10 27% vs 47% (ERAN wins, exceeding even the 40% real-arithmetic ceiling); EMNIST-ByClass 81% vs 80%; EMNIST-Balanced 81% vs 64%

HIGGS width sweep (Table 4): real-arithmetic certified rate stable ~82.5% across widths 128-1024; Std-mode floating-point cost climbs from 0.42pp (width 128) to 5.93pp (width 1024); Measured-Radii mode reclaims 77-83% of that cost, capping it at <=1.4pp; norm computation grows ~4x per width doubling (2.8s to 192.4s)
baselines_compared: Tobler et al.'s formally-verified real-arithmetic Lipschitz certifier (as the target of the counterexample/soundness attack and as the real-arithmetic-kernel validation reference)
Real-arithmetic (unsound) certification (the 'Real' columns/ceiling in Tables 1 and 3)
ERAN (DeepPoly abstract-interpretation domain), the only other publicly available floating-point-sound robustness verifier
